properties.name: Imagawa domain
properties.note: Tōkai hegemon; the house collapsed after Yoshimoto fell at Okehazama (1560).

properties.name: Takeda domain
properties.note: Shingen's Kai–Shinano power base; the house was destroyed by Oda–Tokugawa in 1582.

properties.name: Takeda (Suruga)
properties.note: Suruga, taken from the Imagawa in 1569 and held until the fall of the house in 1582.

properties.name: Saitō domain
properties.note: Mino (Inabayama/Gifu); usurped by Saitō Dōsan in 1542, conquered by Nobunaga in 1567.

properties.name: Oda domain
properties.note: Nobunaga's central-Japan hegemony from the 1568 march on Kyoto to Honnō-ji (1582).

properties.name: Oda (Ōmi)
properties.note: Ōmi, secured after the Azai were destroyed in 1573.

properties.name: Tokugawa (Mikawa–Tōtōmi)
properties.note: Ieyasu broke free of the Imagawa in Mikawa (1560) and added Tōtōmi by c.1570.

properties.name: Tokugawa (Tōkai)
properties.note: The five-province Tōkai domain after the Takeda fell (1582), before the Kantō transfer.

properties.name: Tokugawa (Kantō)
properties.note: In 1590 Hideyoshi moved Ieyasu to the former Hōjō Kantō; he lost the Tōkai entirely. (Awa stayed independent under the Satomi.)

properties.name: Hōjō (max extent)
properties.note: Maximum extent just before the Odawara campaign (under Ujimasa; Ujiyasu died 1571). Awa remained the Satomi’s.

properties.name: Hōjō domain
properties.note: Core Hōjō domains. Captured Kamakura (1512), Edo (1524), Odawara was their base.

properties.name: Satake domain
properties.note: Lords of Hitachi who expanded aggressively under Yoshishige; allied with Hideyoshi but penalized by Ieyasu after Sekigahara.

properties.name: Satomi domain
properties.note: Lords of the Awa (Bōsō) peninsula and great rivals of the Hōjō; fought them at Kōnodai (1538, 1564), kept Awa under Hideyoshi and Ieyasu, and were attainted in 1614. (Contested Kazusa/Shimōsa are not shaded here.)

properties.name: Uesugi (Echigo)
properties.note: Kenshin's Echigo home province. (He did NOT hold Sado — that was the Honma clan, annexed only in 1589 by Kagekatsu.)

properties.name: Uesugi (Etchū)
properties.note: Etchū, secured by 1576; lost to Oda (Sassa Narimasa) around 1582.

properties.name: Uesugi (Noto)
properties.note: Noto, held c.1577–1581 before the Oda offensive (Maeda Toshiie) took it.

properties.name: Sanada domain
properties.note: Master strategists holding Ueda Castle and Numata; famous for defying the Tokugawa twice.

properties.name: Asakura domain
properties.note: Long-lived Echizen house, destroyed by Nobunaga in 1573. (Wakasa was the Wakasa-Takeda’s, occupied by Asakura only c.1568–70.)

properties.name: Hatakeyama (Noto)
properties.note: Noto branch of the prestigious Hatakeyama, held until destroyed by Uesugi Kenshin.

properties.name: Hatakeyama (Kii)
properties.note: Kawachi/Kii branch of the Hatakeyama (Takamasa, then Akitaka); the line ended when Akitaka was killed in 1573.

properties.name: Ikkō-ikki domain
properties.note: Kaga, the "peasant republic" (1488–1580) under the Jōdo-Shinshū league, crushed by Oda (Shibata) in 1580. Fortified enclaves (Ishiyama Hongan-ji in Settsu, Nagashima in Ise, Mikawa to 1564) are not shaded here.

properties.name: Rokkaku domain
properties.note: Southern Ōmi (Kannonji); driven out by Nobunaga in 1568. Shown on the whole province (cannot split N/S Ōmi).

properties.name: Azai domain
properties.note: Northern Ōmi (Odani); independent from the Rokkaku from 1560, destroyed by Nobunaga in 1573. Whole-province overlap with Rokkaku 1560–68.

properties.name: Ashikaga domain
properties.note: The Muromachi shogunate, based in Kyoto. Their direct territorial control was weak, often overshadowed by deputies (Hosokawa, Miyoshi).

properties.name: Miyoshi (Kinai)
properties.note: Nagayoshi’s Kinai hegemony; pushed out when Nobunaga entered Kyoto in 1568.

properties.name: Miyoshi (Shikoku home)
properties.note: The Miyoshi home base in eastern Shikoku, held until Chōsokabe overran it by c.1582.

properties.name: Toyotomi domain
properties.note: Hideyoshi’s core base (Osaka), retained by Hideyori until the Winter/Summer campaigns of 1614–1615.

properties.name: Ukita domain
properties.note: Rose to power through assassinations under Naoie; became core Toyotomi loyalists under Hideie until Sekigahara.

properties.name: Ōuchi domain
properties.note: Dominated western Honshū and N. Kyushu until the Tainei-ji incident (1551); the Mōri inherited the region.

properties.name: Mōri (1555–1566)
properties.note: Chūgoku core territory after Itsukushima (1555) and the Ouchi conquest, prior to subduing the Amago.

properties.name: Mōri (1567–1600)
properties.note: Chūgoku hegemon at its peak expansion after capturing Izumo (1566) until Sekigahara (1600).

properties.name: Amago domain
properties.note: Major daimyo house of the Sanin region based at Gassan-Toda; held out against the Mori until defeated in 1566.

properties.name: Ōtomo domain
properties.note: Northern-Kyushu power based in Bungo; broke after Mimigawa (1578) and the Shimazu advance.

properties.name: Ryūzōji domain
properties.note: Hizen house that rose fast, then collapsed when Takanobu died at Okita-Nawate (1584).

properties.name: Shimazu domain
properties.note: Southern-Kyushu house (briefly nearly all Kyushu c.1586); survived as Satsuma domain.

properties.name: Kōno domain
properties.note: Traditional lords of Iyo Province; subjugated by Hideyoshi in 1585.

properties.name: Chōsokabe (Tosa)
properties.note: Tosa home province, held throughout (and all that remained after Hideyoshi’s 1585 invasion).

properties.name: Chōsokabe (Shikoku peak)
properties.note: Motochika’s brief unification of Shikoku (1583–85), stripped away when he surrendered to Hideyoshi in 1585.

properties.name: Date domain
properties.note: Masamune’s rise: Yonezawa in southern Dewa, plus southern Mutsu — Aizu (from 1589), Iwaki and Rikuzen. Curbed by Hideyoshi 1590–91. The Provinces overlay now draws the true ancient 陸奥/出羽; this domain is built from the finer sub-regions so Date, Nanbu and Mogami stay distinct in the north.

properties.name: Mogami domain
properties.note: Yamagata-based house of central 出羽 (Dewa); allied with Ieyasu at Sekigahara. Shares Dewa with Date’s Yonezawa c.1584–91.

properties.name: Nanbu domain
properties.note: Stable stronghold in the north of ancient 陸奥 (Mutsu) — Sannohe, later Morioka.
